item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The writing is good, which is a good thing for sure, and the concept is promising.

But ultimately it fell short of this potential and I downvoted. It doesn't need just a soft rewrite either, but hard work.
– hide block

1- If this guy is Tantalus-like and this is relevant, there is a need for more Tantalus-like things in the article. It is not that the references are subtle, but that they're not relevant. We know what happened in his past only because we know he is "Venetian Tantalus", which is an OoC info, while IC there is no reason whatsoever to draw the link;

2- You want to keep it light-hearted and humorous, I got it, but at least change the photo for, I don't know, some dude eating Jell-Os. The same for his good English or general lack of characterization, or for the excessive preoccupation of the doctor with unhealthy diet. There is no problem with humorous mainlisters, but this piece does sound as a -J because of the total disregard for formal expectations;

3- Doctor Daley is maybe the worst Foundation employee ever for her complete lack of curiosity. She not only worries only about health and diet, she effectively avoids asking questions that risk his appetite. Lack of answers about a phenomenon is common in the Foundation, but lack of questions is not;

4- Good brands of Maraschino Cherries aren't actually more processed than other canned fruits, they are not fake cherries. Likewise I fail to understand why the need to stop deny him sugar-coated food just because the sugar fall to the ground. More importantly, a test to assert limits for the definition of "food" his anomaly recognizes would be far more interesting than the static list given;

5- Finally, the last note is idiotic. Asking the O-5 Council for permission on nurturing an SCP to health before starting tests is like asking a company's CEO for permission to put on clothes before going to work.

– hide block

TL;DR: There is little interesting bits, unexplored properties, bland characters, unnecessary content. It isn't funny or light-hearted, it is plain stupid. And it can be far better simply by following most of what is already there to a more thorough conclusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He can't be put into a box and forgot. The need for maintenance is, itself, one of the things against Safe. The anomaly requires constant work, instead of just being left undisturbed.

item.title: This.
item.content:encoded:
So much this.

Sadly the weird picture in and of itself is enough to earn a -1 from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why is it Safe? Humanoids are usually Euclid.
If you put it in a box and you don't quite know what happens, that's Euclid.
Plus, anyone who ever read the tale of Tantalus knows that that guy was ruthless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My main issue with this article is that the tone often verges into the informal, and sometimes frankly unprofessional.
Examples
– hide block

I found the use of 'the anomaly' and 'the entity' instead of 'SCP-3048' out of tone, and thus pretty distracting. Also out of tone was Dr. Daley's reaction to SCP-3048's consumption of processed food - yes, she may be a nutritionist, but at the end of the day she's a senior researcher with the SCP Foundation. She would be much more likely to question SCP-3048's intense desire for processed foods and whether it might have a anomalous explanation.
The second interview was not actually an interview - just a conversation. It didn't add anything to the SCP from a clinical standpoint.

Foundation personnel and SCP-3048 alike have expressed distaste for the task of cleaning the resulting mess.

This breaks immersion for me again. Distaste on behalf of either an SCP or on personnel has never stopped the Foundation from doing something. An explanation about the futility of the exercise would be much more in-line.

> I find myself disinclined to gamble this willing cooperation by questioning the entity

Again, unless an SCP becomes violent (and not even then sometimes), information would be of higher priority than cooperation

My word, Agent Matthews has terrible eating habits. — Dr. Daley

In a real scientific setting, this would be extremely unprofessional behaviour and immediately removed from any report

There are a number of other areas in which the tone of the article is… less than clinical. Here are some examples.

Then they started sliding like they were made of squishy ice.

Everyone kept dropping them.

Trying to stick fruit snacks

Having said that, I did quite enjoy the concept of this SCP. I'd love to see it edited to become more in-line with the Foundations standards, but the article was interesting enough as is that I'm upvoting it. I hope to see it go places in the future!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As the entity is incapable of consuming beverages, it must be provided hydration via intravenous line.

Upon initial recovery, the anomaly was described as moderately emaciated. It has since regained 10 kg of body mass under the prolonged care of the Foundation.

It is believed that the anomalous entity remained the sole resident there between the years of 1602 and 2005

Imprisonment beats constant starvation/thirst, and the procedures mostly detail allowed creature comforts and the specific needs of the SCP's diet. He's got everything he wants after about 400 years of presumed isolation and hunger/thirst. Him wanting to escape is very unlikely, given the circumstances.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd go with Safe here; yes he could escape but he's shown absolutely no desire to do so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Purely from the nature of being stuck on an island as long as he had it’s fairto assume that he learned English to alleviate his own boredom.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: imagine writing up the documentation for an anomaly, and like you have the anomaly in your custody and you can take a picture of it, but instead you lean over to your assistant and you're like "YO CHADWICK HEAD DOWN TO WAL-MART AND GET ME A PICTURE OF SOME JELLO"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The entity's primary anomalous trait is its inability to imbibe most forms of food and beverage.

So he can't eat or drink anything, (except some junk food, I guess)? The fact that he doesn't starve or age aren't anomalous effects worth noting? Why not?

Safe

Well I know that arguments about imaginary rules like object classes are pointless, so I am not saying that it especially matters, but this, IMO, can't be Safe. He is a living animal and a sapient human guy who could escape. Also, extensive maintenance is included in the SCPs. This should be Euclid.

I too found the characterization pretty flat, the English too idiomatic and convenient, and the picture irrelevant (I think that Foundation personnel can be assumed to know what a supermarket Jello display looks like).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Though it's not guaranteed to indicate quality, I've noticed articles that are only applicable for the "essential" human tags (alive, humanoid, safe, sapient, sentient) tend to have lacking concepts, and heavily rely on supplements.

Also in agreement that the image is irrelevant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I downvoted instantly on realising the dubious relevance of the image, and nothing thereafter changed my mind. A four-hundred-year-old Venetian who speaks almost entirely in American idiom, and who is otherwise uncharacterised, is not a character I want to spend time with, especially when the anomaly isn't developed beyond the basic initial description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This just got way creepier.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The mythological Tantalus was punished for killing his son and attempting to serve him to the gods as food during a banquet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent work. We never find out about the guy's nephew though. What did tantalus do again?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-458 would either create SCP-3048's perfect pizza (but leave the guy unable to eat it) or create a pizza entirely out of food that SCP-3048 can actually eat. Gummy worm crust with Cheez Whiz sauce and gooey, marshmallow cheese, anyone?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Following this line of thought what about articles of food like the pizza from SCP-458 ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I gotta say that I'm actually very happy with how the first interview log and the overall tone turned out. The second one I admit could use more substance, but the first interview I don't particularly want to change.

I'll definitely write up some additions to the second interview, stuff about the entity's experiences in the past few centuries. I don't think that much would make the difference to you, but it's the only thing I really want to change in this article.

Thank you for the feedback!

Edit: Additions made to the second interview. I'm honestly not very sure if they make the article better or worse.

Edit: Additions removed. Draft reverted. I'm happier with how it is now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They probably could be. Test 3 (dietary supplements) doesn't add any new information or contribute to the humor, and neither does Test 6 (artificial fruit snacks). I could cut those out pretty easily without losing anything.

Thanks for the suggestion!

Edit: Done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think part of the issue is that it drags on so long. The testing logs could probably be shortened, for instance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, English is a global language. This SCP worked at a Greek hotel, but his manager had an Irish last name, and it's a fair bet that a considerable portion of the tourists passing through spoke English.

Out of universe, I didn't much want a language/translation bit interfering with the humor in the piece.

Thanks for contributing to the discussion page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In an early draft, some things about the Tantalus connection were subtle to the point that they were being missed completely. It seems I overcompensated a bit while correcting that.

Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the concept has promise, but you lost me at the interview logs. They felt far too expository and informal. I didn't really feel like the entity was characterized in any unique way. I think making the Foundation interviewer sound a bit more professional and the entity a bit less flippant would go a long way. I'd also give them more time to talk, if he's been around since 1602 there's probably a lot more that could be communicated for characterization purposes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good writing but I find it hard to fathom how someone learned English on a Greek island with enough clarity to use it the way he does with ease(like calling people "the suits"). Why not sick to Italian or Greek which he would be more comfortable with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this is very well done; my one minor nitpick is that, in my opinion, the Tantalus connections were made a bit too obvious. This quote in particular:

"Yes, we know the myth of Tantalus and what he did to merit his punishment"

I feel like it sort of spells out the connection, rather than leaving the reader to discover it. With that said, I'm still giving this a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It makes me really happy to know that those lines in particular and that this article as a whole have accomplished for you what I intended.

Thank you very much for taking the time to comment here on the discussion page!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Is this a hospital or a prison?"
"It's a lot of both and neither."

For some reason, this sounded real to me. The SCP's unmistakable delight at finally being able to eat something was also rather affecting.

Then I remembered why Tantalus was cursed and Fridge Horror struck. Well played, rioludoodle, well played.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The self-replacing cake, eh? Amusing thought. :)

According to the description on SCP-871's page:

If any of these cakes is substantially damaged through any means other than being eaten by a human, including being eaten by a non-human animal, it will be replaced instantaneously.

Thus, if you were to try to feed 871 to 3048, you would get spontaneous cake combustion and a brand new instance of Keter pastry.

Something I find equally entertaining is the thought of cross-testing SCP-3048 with SCP-504.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm curious how SCP-871 would react to something like this occurring? I couldn't help but think of that for some reason.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough.

I'm glad you think it's well-written even if the content doesn't hold your interest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's written well but I don't find it very interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is that so? I'll get right on to fixing it. Thanks!

Edit: Done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love this. Hooray for loopholes!

+ One nitpick
 - But one that borders on creating a plot hole:

Investigation has identified the remains of 51 human individuals in the wreckage of the vessel.

It's actually really rare to find remains, even bones, in extremely old shipwrecks. There are a lot of scavengers on the ocean floor with a "waste not, want not" approach, bodies can become buoyant during decomposition and float away, people can get swept overboard and washed away as the ship sinks, or try to swim to shore like this guy and not make it… its really, really unlikely that they'd find all 51 other people.

It'd actually be better if you just left off that line and the

whose remains have not been found in the wreckage

thing from the next sentence. Raises the same questions without adding the "hey wait a second" trouble, and opens up some additional questions at the same time…

item.title: Re: SCP-3048
item.content:encoded: Should it be? Alright. Fixed it!

item.title: Re: SCP-3048
item.content:encoded:
Hey, rioludoodle, the credits of the image should not be in the article, and instead, should be on the discussion page!

Edit: Well, idk, i think this is valid, but, y'know.

item.title: SCP-3048
item.content:encoded:
Cheers to my second SCP article. I'm much happier with how this one turned out than with my first.

For some inexplicable reason, I imagine this humanoid SCP sounding exactly like Jacksepticeye.

Thank you to Lazar Lyusternik and DarkStuff for giving feedback when this was a concept and to stormbreath, Orsinia, and my good friend IRL Polaneva for critiquing my drafts.

Jello image credit to the user "Jane Mejdahl" on Flickr under the Creative Commons BY-SA 2.0 license.
